items.title: It's Never Personal, Always
items.summary: You ever watch one of them true crime shows? C'mon, I…
items.content_html:
You ever watch one of them true crime shows?

C'mon, I know you have.

They usually start it with the recording of an intense, frantic, 911 call.

Person on the line having the worst day of their life.

Here's the part that gets me though.

When the dispatcher is rude.

Or offended.

A plain ol' jerk.

Victim freaking out.

Operator sighing and annoyed.

Even pissed.

Taking sheet personal.

Basically, "I get you're going through something right now, but can you be nicer? Answer my questions in a less emotional way. How hard is that?"

😠

Dispatchers have an unenviable task every time they clock in.

I get that.

But I think, what a select few may need to work on, is remembering:

It's never about them.

If there's ever a job where it ain't about you, it's as a friggin' 911 operator.

Maybe we just recruit ex-NYC cab drivers and have them be emergency dispatchers nationwide.

They've heard it all.

And they can take it.

They wouldn't even have to move.

Just do that sheet from home.

Distributed workforce!

Shout out to the vast majority of dispatchers who do their job well and with empathy.

☎️

And to old cabbies.

🚕

Thank you for your service.

items.title: We Still Do
items.summary: You ever watch videos about some old retired Marine boxer knocking…
items.content_html:
You ever watch videos about some old retired Marine boxer knocking out some would-be crook?

Like this old fella you wouldn't think much of looking at, just rocking a fool to sleep.

Nighty night.

I would watch them in silent admiration.

Good for you, old man.

You still got it.

Hands are the last thing to go.

And I suppose that's the fate of every man blessed to age past their youth.

When they reach a seasoning where other men no longer find them dangerous upon sight.

And I know that there's a whole school of thought about how being dismissed as weak is actually a benefit, but I dunno.

I dunno.

Thankfully, we have all these force multipliers.

Makes a fight even, even when it ain't.

I'd encourage all of you to look into them, if you haven't.

Follow the law, of course.

...

I do wonder, if I'm honest, if I still got it.

I wonder if you do, too.

...

May you and I never have the need to find out.

But if we do, may we find we still do.

And then some.

🤜🤛

"I need you mountain lion mean."

items.title: Who You Trying To Get Crazy With, Ese?
items.summary: Remember the video game, The Sims? Turns out, we're living in…
items.content_html:
Remember the video game, The Sims?

Turns out, we're living in it.

That's what science wants you to believe.

At least, it's the most likely of scenarios, they say.

The most likely scenario is we are a simulation inside a simulation inside a simulation.

Like how you can play a video game in a video game.

Sims all the way down.

This might make you upset.

But me?

I'm dancin'.

Why?

Because I'm the baddest gamer out here.

At least I was.

When I was a kid in NYC, other kids and adults would watch me play.

Back in the day of standup systems.

At the corner bodegas.

Diners.

Liquor stores.

A lot of them would feed me quarters so I could keep playing.

And a few would stack quarters on the console (normally done to signify you had next), for these fine folks they wanted me to play for hours so they bankrolled me.

Venture backed before I hit double digits.

Where was I?

Sim City.

Oh right.

So life's a vidja game.

And I'm cool with that.

Also, our reality is not locally real.

So there's that, too.

Which is loco.

And, distance is fake.

Or at least, an illusion.

You and something on the edge of the universe can be, most likely are, part of the same dang system.

Which makes no sense.

So let's just take a minute to pause here.

Let go of the controller.

Drop it, I said.

Now, step back.

Take a deep breath.

If this is all a game, how you doing?

Is your PC doing well?

How is Player One?

And if you looked at the world around you as video game characters, maybe the world makes a little more sense.

There's a bit of GTA, Red Dead, Madden, Harvest Moon, and No Man's Sky all out there.

To name a few.

So what's your game?

And how you doing in it?

And, if this is all one, who's the dev?

What studio is behind all this?

🕹

Wow.

items.title: Fill In The
items.summary: After taking a few days off, I thought I might have…
items.content_html:
After taking a few days off, I thought I might have something built up to write about.

Be raring to go.

But here I am, staring at this dang blinking cursor.

As a dad.

As a husband.

I seek certain things for my family.

A good life.

Not an easy life.

A good life.

A good, long, healthy, asymmetric, blessed life.

And that's the part that's hard.

Maybe I default to easy too much.

Because you have to, to some degree, allow the not-so-good times to come.

It's part of equation.

And so I've become a doorman of sorts.

Letting some things in that I would normally not, just because it is part of the learning and growing from experience.

Growing pains.

True wisdom is knowing what to let in, and what to keep out.

So what are the not good things we let in?

...

...

...

I think everyone has the right to decide how they lead their families.

So I'll leave my ways out.

But I will say this:

Everything good has a component of the opposite, err, opposing it.

And what needs to stay out?

This, too, you have to decide.

...

...

...

Think permanent. Uneraseable. Unforgivable. You know, the big uns.

[Looks at you, with furrowed brow:]

Got ID?

👤

items.title: Hearing Aid
items.summary: Sundays are harder days to write. I don't know why. Maybe…
items.content_html:
Sundays are harder days to write.

I don't know why.

Maybe it's because I should take the day off.

A day of rest.

But this is not really laborious.

This is play time.

And so I sits.

So I writes.

Oh, had a miracle happen the other day.

Whole family in our vehicle.

Driving around after some soaking of sun.

After some discussion, we decided to hit the local grocery store last minute so we turned down a side road.

It's that season so lots of construction.

We are waiting at a red at a T. 

Can only go left or right.

We're in the left turn only lane.

Light turns green, I look left and right, all clear, and I start to go.

Now the lane I'm turning onto has two lanes. I usually turn left into the lane on the right, since I'll have to make a right soon after into the store.

Make sense?

So I'm turning and I hear my wife say, calmly but authoritatively, "Turn left. Turn left."

So I turn it tight to the left, getting into the left side of the left lane, and we see a pickup truck fly by us just barely missing our front bumper and right side. 

Lady ran a red.

She never stopped.

Didn't seem to even slow down.

My wife says she gave us a nasty look.

Typical, right?

She had a big dog sticking its head out by her side, so we hypothesize maybe she was distracted. 

But who knows.

Here's the weird part.

Both my son and my wife agree that my wife never said a word.

She remembers gasping and raising her hand up to the right side of her window in an attempt to shield herself.

But didn't say a word.

And my son agrees saying she said nothing.

But what I remember vividly and distinctly is she said, "Turn left. Turn left."

Twice.

And not panicked.

Calm.

Direct.

Matter of factly.

Just do it.

So I did.

Hard.

And the flying dog lady truck missed us.

We had a terrific boost of adrenaline and spent the rest of our day just praising and in a bit of shock.

Thankful.

Near misses are too often and quickly forgotten.

Or at least, not fully appreciated.

I don't want to do that.

So I'll write it down here. 

To remind me to cherish it.

What's weird is this isn't the first time for me.

I had another similar miracle happen to me decades ago.

When I was a teenager.

Also on the road.

A voice told me to stop.

So I did.

But that time, I was in the car all by myself.

items.title: Anyone Feel A Draft?
items.summary: I started writing about two things and erased them both. One…
items.content_html:
I started writing about two things and erased them both.

One was about being vigilant outside as we approach warmer seasons.

The other was about Day 3 of the NFL Draft.

I erased them both.

I don't know why.

I will say, be careful, out there.

And I love Day 3 of the NFL Draft.

I feel like we should have corporate drafts for the Fortune 500.

Scouts in college classrooms.

An academic combine in auditoriums.

Boardrooms watching highlights of volunteer tape.

Talking heads arguing over who Nvidia will take in the first round.

Actually, that sounds terrible.

Or does it already happen?

items.title: Howling
items.summary: A lot of communication is projection. You say things to others…
items.content_html:
A lot of communication is projection.

You say things to others you need to hear.

Others say things they need to.

You ever speak to someone who is just waiting for their turn to talk, though?

I'd like to think I'm a good listener.

And speaking to those type of people, well that's just no fun.

My wife gives me a hard time because years ago, I said something to this famous dude in a rustic but bougie store in the middle of Tennessee.

Truth be told, we heard this guy lived in town and we were visiting the area and so I thought, maybe, just maybe, we could run into him.

Yeah right. What are the chances?

And we hit the town and wandered around, in those days we could only window shop in a town like this.

We were on the look out.

After a few hours, there was only so much loitering looking around we could do, so we left.

On the walk back to our car, we spot a motorcycle with a side car rumbling past.

My wife jokes, but half-serious, "There he is."

And I'm like "Yeah, right."

And she looks closer and confirms, "No, seriously."

And he and his wife walk into the same store we just left!

"Want to go back in?" My wife suggests.

I nod.

We are greeted with a loud (too loud) "Welcome back!" by the store employees, as we sheepishly re-enter.

I play it cool, because I am, but I nod and say yeah, we wanted to look around a bit more. 

But I'm on the hunt.

I eventually make my way over to the celeb's locale, thinking that my wife and little toddler wrapped around my chest would act as a sort of "Don't worry, I'm safe" signal.

"Hey [name], just wanted to shake your hand and say I'm a big fan of you and your show."

He was extremely cordial. Friendly. Complimented my wife's camera. Cooed at my baby boy. Suggested and encouraged us to move to the area (as he had just recently). And rooted for us to keep hustling, telling us it took him five years of picking pitching his stuff daily to finally get a little bite.

He was a genuinely nice person. He treated us as equals.

Now, to the point my wife likes to poke fun at.

During our conversation, I kind of lost what else I was going to say.

So I stopped listening to what he was saying. 

I heard it all. I just wasn't listening.

I was in my brain, thinking of what else I could add, to this person who ended up being much nicer than I thought.

And while I'm searching my internal file folders, I'm hearing how he's discussing how terrible it is that the downtowns in small towns are being ignored. Really beautiful buildings going to ruin. Economy shot. History being forgotten. I mean, he's on a roll with how bad it is.

And when he finishes, I say it:

"That's outstanding."

We all hear it.

But no one sells it.

I thought about correcting myself, but at that point I wrap it up and leave.

After getting a photo.

In hindsight, I'm more embarrassed because I didn't acknowledge the fact that I interrupted his outing with his wife.

I should have apologized to her specifically and thanked her.

After we made our exit, a flock of older ladies formed a circle around the dude, effectively entrapping him.

I think I broke the ice.

Released the flood gates.

I'm sure he was there for hours.

I suppose I should feel bad about that, too.

The lesson here, if there is one, is that I never searched for something to say after that.

I learned to listen.

At least, I hope I did.

Hmm. What did you say?

A work in progress.

🐺

That's outstanding.

items.title: Game Time
items.summary: Here's the deal. It's this dance. What is? Life. You have…
items.content_html:
Here's the deal.

It's this dance.

What is?

Life.

You have to work through this balancing act.

Between today and tomorrow.

Juggling it.

The things you spend on today (not just money) can take away from your future self.

Meaning a dollar wasted today is some multiple of that lost tomorrow.

Opportunity cost.

Opportunity lost.

You can be taking money, health, and sanity out of your future selves' reserves.

It works the other way, too.

Saving for the future self means today goes without.

Somewhat.

Now, obviously the sliding scale has a sweet spot.

One each of us has to determine on their own.

And many of us, sadly, do not have that luxury.

Today is the only day that matters.

It is a day of survival.

I understand.

I lived it.

And so an old(ish) man who plays the long game suddenly realizes he might be in the fourth quarter of a game he thought was only at halftime.

And there's the wisdom wissmart right there.

No one knows how much time is left on the clock.

It takes a degree of faith to play long.

I am all over the place today.

But what I'm getting to is this.

Most of us start off wanting to be great.

But I think most of us really seek to be good.

And there's always time to be good.

Greatness is, under the hood, often a tragedy.

You wouldn't want that life.

You think you do, but you don't.

And I believe, I hope, most of us would love to be known as a good person.

He is a good man.

She is a good woman.

Who do you believe is that?

And, are you?

And what are you doing today, or tomorrow, that shows this?

I'm not asking for me, I'm asking for you.

⏲

Clock's running.

Shoot, pass, set a screen, or call a timeout.

This hasn't been my finest writing, but at least you know I ain't ai.

🏀

items.title: On Repeat
items.summary: "But, that's a sweet song. A sweet song. I hope!" I'm…
items.content_html:
"But, that's a sweet song.

A sweet song.

I hope!"

I'm a surfer.

I mean, not like the kind that catches waves.

Heck no.

I surf the web.

And the sonic.

Some songs, man.

Just hit ya.

Crumple ya.

Humble.

I suppose I need that sometimes.

I'm a one guitar kinda guy.

One piano.

Too many cooks, too much, sometimes.

Sometimes.

One voice.

To dancing in slow-mo.

Riding big waves.

And listening to them crash.

Into you.

👋

items.title: Painting Over the Ugly Parts
items.summary: 🎵 "I watched the proverbial sunrise Coming up over the Pacific…
items.content_html:
🎵 "I watched the proverbial sunrise

Coming up over the Pacific and

You might think I'm losing my mind

But I will shy away from the specifics." 🎶

There's an old song most people hated.

It was a guy speaking over a nice beat.

A commencement speech.

Just talking.

Giving advice.

And there's a line in it about living in New York City once, but leaving it before you it makes you hard.

And to live in Northern California once but leave before it makes you soft.

And it follows another bit of advice I remember:

New Yorkers are not nice, but they are kind.

Californians are nice, but they are not kind.

These are harsh generalizations, but that's what old(ish) people do.

Makes things easier to process.

Digest.

You might call it prejudice, they call it experience.

I call it wisdom.

Or, wissmart.

Tee hee.

That's never going to catch on.

Hrmph.

Man, the right song can make a sad man happy.

And a happy one sad.

Ain't that something?

This time machine, man.

It runs on sound waves.

⏳🎼🎢

I made a mix.

Check this out.

📀

😎

🚘
